dialogue.ru: Звучит как хороший баланс.

b2-l1.title: Wishes & Regrets
b2-l1.pattern: I wish + past simple/past perfect / If only...
b2-l1.explanation: "I wish" expresses a desire for something to be different — for the present, it pairs with past simple ("I wish I had more time"), and for the past, with past perfect ("I wish I had studied more"). "If only" works the same way but adds more emotional emphasis.
b2-l1.fact: "I wish" is one of the few remaining places in English where the old subjunctive mood survives clearly — "I wish I were taller" (not "was") is still considered the more correct form in careful writing, even though "was" is extremely common in casual speech.

dialogue.en: I wish I had more free time these days.
dialogue.ru: Хотела бы я иметь сейчас больше свободного времени.

dialogue.en: If only we hadn't taken on so many projects at once.
dialogue.ru: Если бы мы только не взялись за столько проектов одновременно.

dialogue.en: I wish I had said no to the last one.
dialogue.ru: Жаль, что я не отказалась от последнего.

dialogue.en: Well, we'll know better next time.
dialogue.ru: Ну, в следующий раз будем умнее.

b2-l2.title: Mixed Conditionals
b2-l2.pattern: past unreal cause -> present unreal result (or reverse)
b2-l2.explanation: Mixed conditionals combine two different time frames in one sentence: "If I had taken that job, I would be rich now" (past condition, present result). This is more advanced than the standard conditionals because the "if" clause and the main clause don't have to match in time.
b2-l2.fact: Mixed conditionals are entirely rule-based, not memorized as fixed phrases — fluent speakers construct them on the fly by mixing and matching the "if" half of Third Conditional with the result half of Second Conditional (or vice versa), which is why they feel intuitive once the underlying logic clicks.

dialogue.en: If I had learned to code earlier, I would have a different career now.
dialogue.ru: Если бы я раньше научился программировать, у меня была бы сейчас другая карьера.

dialogue.en: If you weren't so busy now, you could start learning today.
dialogue.ru: Если бы ты сейчас не был так занят, ты мог бы начать учиться уже сегодня.

dialogue.en: True. If I hadn't procrastinated so much, I'd be further along already.
dialogue.ru: Верно. Если бы я так не откладывал, я бы уже сильно продвинулся.

dialogue.en: It's never too late to start.
dialogue.ru: Начать никогда не поздно.

b2-l3.title: Advanced Passive
b2-l3.pattern: It is believed that... / He is said to have...
b2-l3.explanation: Advanced passive constructions distance a claim from its source: "It is believed that prices will rise" avoids saying exactly who believes it. "He is said to have left the country" reports a rumor without confirming it — extremely common in news and formal reporting.
b2-l3.fact: This construction lets English report gossip or unverified claims while sounding neutral and objective — "is rumored to", "is thought to", "is alleged to" all follow the identical pattern, and journalists rely on this structure heavily to report claims they can't personally verify.

dialogue.en: It is believed that the company will announce layoffs soon.
dialogue.ru: Считается, что компания скоро объявит о сокращениях.

dialogue.en: The manager is said to have already resigned.
dialogue.ru: Говорят, что менеджер уже подал в отставку.

dialogue.en: It's thought that a new director will be appointed next month.
dialogue.ru: Предполагается, что новый директор будет назначен в следующем месяце.

dialogue.en: We'll find out soon enough, I suppose.
dialogue.ru: Что ж, полагаю, скоро всё узнаем.

b2-l4.title: Emphasis & Inversion
b2-l4.pattern: Never have I... / Not only did she..., but also...
b2-l4.explanation: Inversion moves the auxiliary verb before the subject for dramatic emphasis, mostly with negative or restrictive words at the start of a sentence: "Never have I seen such a mess." This is a formal, literary pattern — normal speech would just say "I have never seen such a mess."
b2-l4.fact: Inversion for emphasis is grammatically identical in structure to forming a question ("Have I ever..." vs "Never have I...") — this is deliberate: English borrows the question-word-order pattern specifically because it already signals "something unusual is happening here" to a native speaker's ear.

dialogue.en: Never have I seen such dedication to a project.
dialogue.ru: Никогда я не видел такой преданности проекту.

dialogue.en: Not only did she finish early, but she also helped the others.
dialogue.ru: Она не только закончила раньше, но и помогла остальным.

dialogue.en: Rarely do we get such reliable team members.
dialogue.ru: Редко нам попадаются такие надёжные члены команды.

dialogue.en: We should definitely recognize her effort.
dialogue.ru: Нам определённо стоит отметить её усилия.

b2-l5.title: Society & Technology
b2-l5.pattern: advanced abstract vocabulary for discussing trends
b2-l5.explanation: Discussing society and technology at an advanced level relies on abstract nouns ("innovation", "inequality", "automation") and hedged claims ("tends to", "is likely to", "arguably") rather than simple present-tense facts. This vocabulary shift — from concrete to abstract — marks the transition from intermediate to advanced English.
b2-l5.fact: Many of these abstract nouns are formed by adding Latin/French suffixes (-tion, -ity, -ism) to simpler root words — "equal" becomes "equality", "innovate" becomes "innovation" — recognizing this pattern lets learners guess the meaning of hundreds of advanced words they've never seen before.

dialogue.en: Automation is arguably reshaping entire industries.
dialogue.ru: Автоматизация, можно сказать, полностью меняет целые отрасли.

dialogue.en: It tends to create new jobs while eliminating old ones.
dialogue.ru: Она, как правило, создаёт новые рабочие места, устраняя старые.

dialogue.en: The bigger question is inequality — who actually benefits.
dialogue.ru: Более важный вопрос — неравенство: кто на самом деле выигрывает.

dialogue.en: That's exactly what policymakers are struggling with.
dialogue.ru: Именно с этим сейчас и борются политики.

b2-l6.title: Advanced Linking Words
b2-l6.explanation: Advanced linking words replace simpler connectors ("but", "also", "so") in formal writing and speech: "nonetheless" instead of "but still", "consequently" instead of "so". They're interchangeable in meaning but signal a more formal, academic register.
b2-l6.fact: "Notwithstanding" can grammatically appear both before AND after the phrase it modifies ("notwithstanding the risks" or "the risks notwithstanding") — one of the only English connectors with this flexible word order, a leftover from its legal-document origins.

dialogue.en: The results were disappointing; nonetheless, we learned a lot.
dialogue.ru: Результаты разочаровали; тем не менее, мы многому научились.

dialogue.en: Furthermore, we now have data to guide the next attempt.
dialogue.ru: Более того, теперь у нас есть данные для следующей попытки.

dialogue.en: Consequently, I think we should revise the whole approach.
dialogue.ru: Следовательно, думаю, нам стоит пересмотреть весь подход.

dialogue.en: Agreed. The risks notwithstanding, it's worth trying again.
dialogue.ru: Согласен. Несмотря на риски, стоит попробовать снова.

b2plus-l1.title: Hedging & Softening
b2plus-l1.pattern: I feel like... / It might be worth... / I don't want to sound...
b2plus-l1.explanation: Hedging softens a statement to sound less blunt or more diplomatic — crucial for disagreeing politely or giving criticism in English-speaking professional culture. Direct opinions that sound normal in Russian can come across as unnecessarily harsh in English without this softening layer.
b2plus-l1.fact: English speakers hedge so habitually that entire conversations can consist mostly of softened statements — overusing direct language ("You're wrong", "That's a bad idea") without any hedging is one of the most common ways non-native speakers unintentionally sound rude in English, even with perfect grammar.

dialogue.en: I feel like this plan might have a few issues.
dialogue.ru: Мне кажется, у этого плана могут быть кое-какие проблемы.

dialogue.en: I don't want to sound negative, but I share that concern.
dialogue.ru: Не хочу показаться пессимистом, но я разделяю это беспокойство.

dialogue.en: It might be worth revisiting the budget section.
dialogue.ru: Возможно, стоит ещё раз пересмотреть раздел бюджета.

dialogue.en: That sounds reasonable to me.
dialogue.ru: Мне это кажется разумным.

b2plus-l2.title: Idiomatic Collocations
b2plus-l2.pattern: words that pair naturally (make a decision, take a risk)
b2plus-l2.explanation: Collocations are word pairings that native speakers use automatically but which don't follow a predictable logical rule: you "make" a decision but "take" a risk, never the reverse. These pairings have to be learned individually, since translating word-for-word from Russian often produces the wrong pairing.
b2plus-l2.fact: Native speakers can spot a non-native collocation error almost instantly, even when the sentence is otherwise grammatically perfect — "do a decision" instead of "make a decision" immediately signals non-native speech, which is why collocation mastery is often considered the real marker of fluency.

dialogue.en: We need to make a decision by Friday.
dialogue.ru: Нам нужно принять решение к пятнице.

dialogue.en: I'm willing to take a risk on this one.
dialogue.ru: Я готова пойти на риск в этом деле.

dialogue.en: Let's pay attention to the smaller details too.
dialogue.ru: Давай обратим внимание и на мелкие детали тоже.

dialogue.en: Agreed. It's worth taking the time to do it right.
dialogue.ru: Согласна. Стоит потратить время, чтобы сделать это правильно.

b2plus-l3.title: Narrative Tense Mixing
b2plus-l3.pattern: past simple + past continuous + past perfect together
b2plus-l3.explanation: Real storytelling in English weaves together multiple past tenses in the same narrative: past simple for the main events, past continuous for background action, past perfect for things that happened earlier still. Mastering this mix is what makes a story sound naturally sequenced instead of a flat list of facts.
b2plus-l3.fact: This layered tense system lets English tell a story with implicit timing that some languages need explicit words for — "I walked in; she had already left" instantly communicates the order of events through verb form alone, with no need for a word like "before" or "earlier."

dialogue.en: I was walking home when it started raining.
dialogue.ru: Я шла домой, когда начался дождь.

dialogue.en: Had you already left the office by then?
dialogue.ru: Ты к тому моменту уже ушла из офиса?

dialogue.en: Yes, I had left about ten minutes earlier.
dialogue.ru: Да, я ушла минут за десять до этого.

dialogue.en: That's unlucky timing.
dialogue.ru: Неудачное совпадение по времени.

b2plus-l4.title: Formal Register
b2plus-l4.pattern: I would be grateful if... / Please find attached... / We regret to inform you...
b2plus-l4.explanation: Formal register replaces everyday phrasing with more elaborate, indirect constructions — used in professional emails, official letters, and business correspondence. "Could you send it?" becomes "I would be grateful if you could send it," adding distance and courtesy through extra words.
b2plus-l4.fact: Formal register in English favors longer, more Latin-derived words and more indirect requests, while casual register favors short Germanic-root words and directness — this split traces back to the historical layering of French/Latin vocabulary (formal, prestigious) over the original Old English vocabulary (everyday, direct) after the Norman Conquest.

dialogue.en: I would be grateful if you could confirm the details by Friday.
dialogue.ru: Буду признателен, если вы подтвердите детали к пятнице.

dialogue.en: Certainly. Please find the revised document attached.
dialogue.ru: Конечно. Пересмотренный документ прикреплён к письму.

dialogue.en: We regret to inform you that the meeting has been postponed.
dialogue.ru: С сожалением сообщаем, что встреча была перенесена.

dialogue.en: Understood. Thank you for letting us know.
dialogue.ru: Понятно. Спасибо, что сообщили.

b2plus-l5.title: Attitude & Emotion
b2plus-l5.pattern: I can't help but... / It strikes me as... / I'm inclined to think...
b2plus-l5.explanation: These phrases express nuanced emotional reactions and attitudes rather than plain statements of fact: "I can't help but feel..." signals an involuntary reaction, "it strikes me as..." introduces a subjective impression, softer than a flat claim.
b2plus-l5.fact: "Can't help but" is a double-negative-feeling structure that's grammatically positive — literally "cannot avoid [doing]" — and it's one of many English idioms where the literal words don't fully predict the actual meaning, making it a common source of confusion even for advanced learners.

dialogue.en: I can't help but worry about the deadline.
dialogue.ru: Не могу не волноваться из-за сроков.

dialogue.en: It strikes me as manageable, honestly.
dialogue.ru: Мне это кажется вполне выполнимым, если честно.

dialogue.en: I'm inclined to think we need more people on the team.
dialogue.ru: Я склоняюсь к мысли, что нам нужно больше людей в команде.

dialogue.en: That might be worth proposing to management.
dialogue.ru: Возможно, стоит предложить это руководству.

b2plus-l6.title: Nuanced Modality
b2plus-l6.pattern: should have / needn't have / could have (unfulfilled possibilities)
b2plus-l6.explanation: These modal combinations describe things that didn't happen, each with a distinct emotional flavor: "should have" (regret, it was the right thing to do), "needn't have" (it happened but was unnecessary), "could have" (it was possible but didn't occur). Choosing the wrong one changes the whole meaning of a sentence.
b2plus-l6.fact: "Needn't have done" is genuinely rare and often replaced by "didn't need to" in casual speech, but the two aren't quite identical: "needn't have brought an umbrella" implies you DID bring one unnecessarily, while "didn't need to bring an umbrella" is neutral about whether you actually brought one.

dialogue.en: You should have told me you were running late.
dialogue.ru: Тебе стоило сказать мне, что ты опаздываешь.

dialogue.en: I know, I'm sorry. I needn't have rushed at the end, either.
dialogue.ru: Знаю, извини. И в конце мне не нужно было так спешить.

dialogue.en: You could have just called instead of texting.
dialogue.ru: Ты мог просто позвонить, а не писать сообщение.

dialogue.en: You're right. I'll do that next time.
dialogue.ru: Ты права. В следующий раз так и сделаю.

c1-l1.title: Academic & Analytical Language
c1-l1.pattern: the data suggest(s) / it should be noted that / this raises the question of
c1-l1.explanation: Academic English favors cautious, evidence-based phrasing over direct claims — "the data suggest" instead of "this proves", "it should be noted" instead of bluntly stating a caveat. This careful hedging is a hallmark of scholarly writing, distinguishing it from confident everyday assertions.
c1-l1.fact: "Data" is technically the plural of "datum" in strict academic style ("the data suggest", not "suggests"), though in modern everyday English, "data" is almost universally treated as singular — this split between formal academic convention and common usage is a genuinely live disagreement even among native speakers.

dialogue.en: The data suggest a clear correlation, though causation remains unproven.
dialogue.ru: Данные указывают на явную корреляцию, хотя причинность остаётся недоказанной.

dialogue.en: It should be noted that our sample size was quite small.
dialogue.ru: Стоит отметить, что наш объём выборки был довольно небольшим.

dialogue.en: Exactly. This raises the question of whether a larger study is needed.
dialogue.ru: Именно. Это поднимает вопрос о необходимости более масштабного исследования.

dialogue.en: I'll include that limitation in the conclusion.
dialogue.ru: Я укажу это ограничение в заключении.

c1-l2.title: Rhetorical Devices & Persuasion
c1-l2.explanation: Persuasive English often uses structural devices rather than just strong vocabulary: rhetorical questions (asked to make a point, not to get an answer), tricolon (grouping ideas in threes for rhythm — "government of the people, by the people, for the people"), and deliberate repetition for emphasis.
c1-l2.fact: The rule of three (tricolon) is so deeply embedded in English rhetoric that speakers instinctively expect a third item once two have been given — ending a list at two items can feel unfinished to an English-speaking audience, even when there's nothing more to say.

dialogue.en: Isn't it time we asked ourselves what really matters here?
dialogue.ru: Не пора ли нам спросить себя, что здесь действительно важно?

dialogue.en: That's a fair question, honestly.
dialogue.ru: Честно говоря, это справедливый вопрос.

dialogue.en: We need courage, clarity, and commitment — nothing less.
dialogue.ru: Нам нужны смелость, ясность и решимость — не меньше.

dialogue.en: Put that way, it's hard to disagree.
dialogue.ru: В такой формулировке трудно не согласиться.

c1-l3.title: Subtle Contrast & Concession
c1-l3.pattern: granted, ... / that said, ... / while it's true that...
c1-l3.explanation: Advanced concession structures acknowledge an opposing point before pivoting back to your own argument — "Granted, the cost is high, but the long-term savings justify it." This move (conceding, then countering) is a persuasive technique that shows you've considered the other side.
c1-l3.fact: Skilled arguers deliberately concede minor, less important points to sound more credible before defending the point they actually care about — audiences trust speakers more when they openly acknowledge some validity in the opposing view, a well-documented effect in rhetoric and negotiation research alike.

dialogue.en: Granted, the new system has a learning curve.
dialogue.ru: Согласен, у новой системы есть период освоения.

dialogue.en: That said, the long-term efficiency gains seem worth it.
dialogue.ru: Тем не менее, долгосрочный выигрыш в эффективности, похоже, того стоит.

dialogue.en: While it's true that training takes time, we can phase it in.
dialogue.ru: Хотя обучение действительно требует времени, мы можем внедрять это постепенно.

dialogue.en: Agreed. Let's propose a gradual rollout.
dialogue.ru: Согласен. Давай предложим постепенное внедрение.

c1-l4.title: Professional Negotiation Language
c1-l4.pattern: We'd be willing to... / provided that... / on the condition that...
c1-l4.explanation: Negotiation English is precise about conditions and mutual concessions: "We'd be willing to lower the price, provided that you commit to a longer contract" clearly links one concession to a specific condition. This precision avoids ambiguity in agreements where the exact terms matter.
c1-l4.fact: "Provided that" and "on the condition that" are functionally interchangeable, but "provided that" sounds slightly less formal and is more common in spoken negotiation, while "on the condition that" appears more often in written contracts — a subtle register distinction skilled negotiators use deliberately.

dialogue.en: We'd be willing to sign today, provided that the price includes delivery.
dialogue.ru: Мы готовы подписать сегодня, при условии, что цена включает доставку.

dialogue.en: That's acceptable, on the condition that payment is made within thirty days.
dialogue.ru: Это приемлемо, при условии, что оплата будет произведена в течение тридцати дней.

dialogue.en: Agreed. Let's put that in writing.
dialogue.ru: Согласны. Давайте оформим это письменно.

dialogue.en: I'll have the contract ready within the hour.
dialogue.ru: Договор будет готов в течение часа.

c1-l5.title: Abstract & Philosophical Discussion
c1-l5.pattern: abstract nouns + hedged claims (arguably, in a sense, to some extent)
c1-l5.explanation: Philosophical English discussion relies heavily on abstract nouns (freedom, meaning, identity) combined with careful hedges that avoid overclaiming: "arguably", "in a sense", "to some extent". These qualifiers signal intellectual humility — a claim presented as absolute truth reads as naive in this register.
c1-l5.fact: The word "arguably" is one of the most overused hedges in academic and journalistic English precisely because it lets a writer make a strong claim while technically leaving room for disagreement — style guides frequently warn against overusing it, yet it remains extremely common in exactly this kind of abstract discussion.

dialogue.en: Freedom, arguably, means something different to every generation.
dialogue.ru: Свобода, можно сказать, для каждого поколения значит что-то своё.

dialogue.en: In a sense, that's true of most abstract concepts.
dialogue.ru: В каком-то смысле это верно для большинства абстрактных понятий.

dialogue.en: To some extent, our sense of identity is shaped by circumstance.
dialogue.ru: В какой-то степени наше чувство идентичности формируется обстоятельствами.

dialogue.en: That's a fair point, though I'd argue choice matters just as much.
dialogue.ru: Справедливое замечание, хотя я бы сказал, что выбор играет не меньшую роль.

c1-l6.title: Advanced Discourse Markers
c1-l6.pattern: that said / on the flip side / needless to say / in any case
c1-l6.explanation: These markers manage the flow and structure of a sophisticated argument — signaling a shift, a counterpoint, or a return to the main thread. "That said" pivots after a concession, "on the flip side" introduces a contrasting perspective, "needless to say" flags something the speaker assumes is obvious.
c1-l6.fact: Fluent use of discourse markers is one of the clearest signals of near-native proficiency — they don't add new information to a sentence, but they guide the listener's expectations moment to moment, and their absence (or misuse) is often what makes otherwise grammatically correct advanced English sound slightly foreign.

dialogue.en: The results were mixed. That said, the overall trend is positive.
dialogue.ru: Результаты были неоднозначными. Тем не менее, общая тенденция положительная.

dialogue.en: On the flip side, competitors are moving faster than expected.
dialogue.ru: С другой стороны, конкуренты действуют быстрее, чем ожидалось.

dialogue.en: Needless to say, we'll need to adjust the strategy.
dialogue.ru: Само собой разумеется, нам нужно будет скорректировать стратегию.

dialogue.en: In any case, let's reconvene next week with a revised plan.
dialogue.ru: В любом случае, давайте соберёмся на следующей неделе с пересмотренным планом.

a1-l13.title: Plurals & This/That/These/Those
a1-l13.explanation: "This" and "these" point to things close to you; "that" and "those" point to things farther away. Most English nouns just add -s for the plural (book → books), but a handful of very common ones change completely: child → children, man → men, woman → women. There's no shortcut — you just have to memorize these few.
a1-l13.fact: English used to have far more irregular plurals centuries ago, but most got "regularized" to the simple -s pattern over time. The ones that survived — child/children, man/men, woman/women, foot/feet — are among the oldest and most frequently used words in the language, which is exactly why they resisted the change.

dialogue.en: Whose books are these?
dialogue.ru: Чьи это книги?

dialogue.en: These are my sister's books. That box over there is mine.
dialogue.ru: Это книги моей сестры. А та коробка вон там — моя.

dialogue.en: Are those your children in the photo?
dialogue.ru: Это твои дети на фото?

dialogue.en: Yes, this is my son and that is my daughter.
dialogue.ru: Да, это мой сын, а это моя дочь.

a1-l14.title: Can & Can't — Ability
a1-l14.explanation: "Can" is a modal verb, which means it never changes form — no matter who the subject is, it's always just "can" (not "cans" or "canning"). It's followed directly by the base form of the verb, with no "to": "I can swim", not "I can to swim". The negative is "can't" (or "cannot").
a1-l14.fact: "Can" is one of the oldest words in English, coming from an Old English verb that originally meant "to know" — so "I can swim" literally meant something closer to "I know how to swim" a thousand years ago, which is still exactly the meaning it carries today.

dialogue.en: Can you help me carry this table?
dialogue.ru: Можешь помочь мне поднести этот стол?

dialogue.en: Sorry, I can't right now — my back hurts. Can you ask Timur?
dialogue.ru: Извини, сейчас не могу — у меня болит спина. Можешь попросить Тимура?

dialogue.en: Good idea. He can lift heavy things easily.
dialogue.ru: Хорошая идея. Он легко поднимает тяжёлые вещи.

a1-l15.title: Present Continuous
a1-l15.explanation: Use the present continuous for things happening right now, at this exact moment. It's built from "to be" plus the verb's -ing form: I am reading, she is cooking, they are talking. This is different from present simple, which describes habits and general facts ("I read every day") rather than what's happening this second.
a1-l15.fact: The -ing form is sometimes called the "present participle", and English is unusual among European languages for using it this heavily to mark an ongoing action — many languages just use their normal present tense for both habits and things happening right now.

dialogue.en: Where's Bekzat? I need to talk to him.
dialogue.ru: Где Бекзат? Мне нужно с ним поговорить.

dialogue.en: He's in the kitchen. He is cooking dinner right now.
dialogue.ru: Он на кухне. Сейчас он готовит ужин.

dialogue.en: And what are you doing?
dialogue.ru: А ты что делаешь?

dialogue.en: I'm waiting for the water to boil.
dialogue.ru: Жду, пока закипит вода.

a1-l16.title: Imperatives & Directions
a1-l16.pattern: base verb (+ please) · Don't + base verb
a1-l16.explanation: The imperative gives instructions or commands, and it's the simplest verb form in English: just the base verb, with no subject and no "to". "Open the window." "Turn left." Adding "please" makes it politer. To tell someone NOT to do something, put "Don't" in front: "Don't forget your keys."
a1-l16.fact: Because the imperative drops the subject entirely, it's the one place in English where a full sentence can be a single word — "Stop!", "Wait!", "Listen!" — which is why it's often the fastest form learners pick up for everyday survival phrases.

dialogue.en: Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?
dialogue.ru: Извините, как пройти до вокзала?

dialogue.en: Go straight, then turn left at the bank. Don't turn right — that street is closed.
dialogue.ru: Идите прямо, потом поверните налево у банка. Не поворачивайте направо — та улица закрыта.

dialogue.en: Thank you! And is it far?
dialogue.ru: Спасибо! А это далеко?

dialogue.en: No, just wait five minutes and you'll see it.
dialogue.ru: Нет, просто подождите пять минут, и вы её увидите.

a1-l17.title: Telling the Time
a1-l17.pattern: It is [hour] o'clock · half past / quarter to / quarter past
a1-l17.explanation: To tell time in English, state the hour first, then the minutes: "half past four" (4:30), "quarter to six" (5:45), "quarter past seven" (7:15). For exact hours, add "o'clock": "three o'clock". Events happen "at" a time: "at seven o'clock", "at noon", "at midnight".
a1-l17.fact: "Half past" and "quarter to/past" come from clock faces literally being divided into quarters by their hands — a tradition that goes back centuries before digital clocks existed, which is why English still describes time in fractions of an hour rather than just the raw numbers.

dialogue.en: What time does the shop open?
dialogue.ru: Во сколько открывается магазин?

dialogue.en: It opens at nine in the morning and closes at eight in the evening.
dialogue.ru: Открывается в девять утра, закрывается в восемь вечера.

dialogue.en: It's already quarter to nine — let's go!
dialogue.ru: Уже без пятнадцати девять — пойдём!

dialogue.en: Don't worry, we have time. The bus arrives in ten minutes.
dialogue.ru: Не волнуйся, у нас есть время. Автобус приедет через десять минут.

a1-l18.title: Adjectives & Opposites
a1-l18.explanation: Adjectives describe nouns and, in English, never change form to match them — one word works for singular, plural, masculine, or feminine: "a big house", "big houses". Learning adjectives in opposite pairs (big/small, hot/cold, clean/dirty) is one of the fastest ways to double your descriptive vocabulary at once.
a1-l18.fact: Unlike Russian, English adjectives have no gender or case endings at all — "big" is exactly the same word whether it describes a house, a dog, or an idea, which is one reason English adjectives are usually easier to learn than Russian ones, even though English word order is stricter.

dialogue.en: How's your new apartment?
dialogue.ru: Как твоя новая квартира?

dialogue.en: It's small, but very clean and quiet. The old one was big but noisy.
dialogue.ru: Маленькая, но очень чистая и тихая. Старая была большая, но шумная.

dialogue.en: That sounds better. Is the kitchen nice?
dialogue.ru: Звучит лучше. А кухня хорошая?

dialogue.en: Yes, and the food from the restaurant downstairs is delicious.
dialogue.ru: Да, и еда из ресторана внизу очень вкусная.

a2-l13.title: Past Continuous
a2-l13.explanation: Past continuous paints the background of a story: an action already in progress at a specific past moment. It's often paired with past simple to show an interruption — the long action uses past continuous, the short interrupting one uses past simple: "I was reading (long) when the phone rang (short)." It's also used for two things happening at once: "While she was studying, he was cooking."
a2-l13.fact: This tense is why English storytelling often "sets the scene" before the action starts — a sentence like "The rain was falling and the wind was blowing" uses past continuous purely to build atmosphere before the actual event of the story happens in past simple.

dialogue.en: You look tired. What were you doing last night?
dialogue.ru: Ты выглядишь уставшей. Что ты делала вчера вечером?

dialogue.en: I was writing a report until midnight. My sister was watching TV next to me the whole time.
dialogue.ru: Я писал отчёт до полуночи. Сестра всё это время смотрела рядом телевизор.

dialogue.en: Did she help you?
dialogue.ru: Она тебе помогла?

dialogue.en: No, but at least it wasn't completely silent while I was working.
dialogue.ru: Нет, но хотя бы не было совсем тихо, пока я работал.

a2-l14.title: Used to — Past Habits
a2-l14.pattern: used to + base verb · didn't use to + base verb
a2-l14.explanation: "Used to" describes a habit or state that was true in the past but has now changed — either an action you repeated ("I used to play tennis") or a state that was different ("There used to be a cinema here"). The negative and question forms drop the -d: "I didn't use to smoke", "Did you use to live here?"
a2-l14.fact: "Used to" is easy to confuse with "to be used to" (meaning accustomed to something), which looks almost identical but has a completely different grammar — "used to" is always followed by a base verb, while "be used to" is followed by -ing, as in "I'm used to waking up early."

dialogue.en: This street looks so different now.
dialogue.ru: Эта улица теперь выглядит совсем по-другому.

dialogue.en: I know — there used to be a small market right here. We used to buy bread from that old woman every morning.
dialogue.ru: Знаю — раньше прямо здесь был маленький рынок. Мы каждое утро покупали хлеб у той старушки.

dialogue.en: Did you use to live nearby?
dialogue.ru: Ты раньше жила поблизости?

dialogue.en: Yes, for ten years. I didn't use to like this neighborhood, but now I miss it.
dialogue.ru: Да, десять лет. Раньше я не любила этот район, а теперь скучаю по нему.

a2-l15.title: Articles: a/an/the vs No Article
a2-l15.pattern: a/an (one, unspecific) · the (specific, already known) · no article (general plurals/uncountables)
a2-l15.explanation: Use "a/an" the first time you mention something, or when it's just one of many ("I have a cat"). Use "the" once both speakers know exactly which one you mean ("The cat is sleeping" — we already know which cat). Use no article at all for general statements about plural or uncountable things ("Water is important", "Children like playing outside").
a2-l15.fact: Russian has no articles at all, which is exactly why this topic is famously one of the hardest for Russian speakers — the choice isn't really about grammar rules so much as about what information the listener already has, a distinction Russian expresses through word order and context instead.

dialogue.en: I saw a really interesting documentary yesterday.
dialogue.ru: Вчера я посмотрела очень интересный документальный фильм.

dialogue.en: Oh, was it the one about water in Central Asia?
dialogue.ru: О, это тот, про воду в Центральной Азии?

dialogue.en: Yes! It said water is becoming a serious problem here.
dialogue.ru: Да! Там говорилось, что вода становится здесь серьёзной проблемой.

dialogue.en: I'd like to watch it too. Can you send me the link?
dialogue.ru: Я тоже хотел бы его посмотреть. Можешь отправить мне ссылку?

a2-l16.title: Question Tags
a2-l16.pattern: positive statement + negative tag? · negative statement + positive tag?
a2-l16.explanation: A question tag is a mini-question added to the end of a statement to check or confirm information: "You're a student, aren't you?" The rule is simple but strict: a positive statement gets a negative tag, and a negative statement gets a positive one — and the tag always matches the main verb's tense (is→isn't, can→can't, has→hasn't).
a2-l16.fact: Native speakers rely heavily on the melody of the voice with question tags — a rising tone means you're genuinely unsure and asking, while a falling tone means you're almost certain and just want agreement, so the same words can sound like a real question or a casual comment depending only on intonation.

dialogue.en: You've finished the report, haven't you?
dialogue.ru: Ты закончил отчёт, не так ли?

dialogue.en: Almost. You aren't in a hurry, are you?
dialogue.ru: Почти. Ты же не торопишься, да?

dialogue.en: A little — the meeting starts soon, doesn't it?
dialogue.ru: Немного — встреча ведь скоро начинается, да?

dialogue.en: Right. Give me five minutes, will you?
dialogue.ru: Верно. Дай мне пять минут, хорошо?

a2-l17.title: Suggestions & Invitations
a2-l17.pattern: Let's + base verb · Why don't we/you...? · How about + -ing? · Would you like to...?
a2-l17.explanation: English has several everyday phrases for proposing an activity, each slightly different in tone. "Let's..." includes yourself in the plan. "Why don't we/you...?" sounds like friendly advice. "How about...?" is followed by -ing and feels casual. "Would you like to...?" is the most polite, often used with people you don't know well.
a2-l17.fact: "Let's" is a contraction of "let us", but it has become so fixed as its own word that native speakers almost never expand it back — saying the full "let us go" instead of "let's go" actually sounds formal or even slightly dramatic in everyday speech.

dialogue.en: Let's go to that new café after work.
dialogue.ru: Давай пойдём в то новое кафе после работы.

dialogue.en: Good idea. Or how about trying the restaurant on the corner instead?
dialogue.ru: Хорошая идея. А может, попробуем ресторан на углу?

dialogue.en: Why don't we ask Dina too? Would you like to invite her?
dialogue.ru: Почему бы нам не позвать и Дину? Хочешь её пригласить?

dialogue.en: Sure, I'll message her now.
dialogue.ru: Конечно, напишу ей сейчас же.

a2-l18.title: Prepositions of Time: in/on/at
a2-l18.pattern: in (months, years, seasons) · on (days, dates) · at (clock times, noon/midnight)
a2-l18.explanation: Think of these three prepositions as zooming in from big to small. "In" covers long periods: months, years, seasons ("in May", "in 2020", "in winter"). "On" covers single days and dates ("on Monday", "on the fifth of June"). "At" covers precise points in time: clock times, plus the fixed expressions "at noon", "at midnight", and "at night".
a2-l18.fact: "At night" is the one exception that breaks the otherwise tidy in/on/at pattern — every other part of the day uses "in" (in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening), but night alone takes "at", a quirk that goes back to "night" once being treated as a single point in time rather than a stretch of one.

dialogue.en: When's your exam?
dialogue.ru: Когда у тебя экзамен?

dialogue.en: On Friday, at ten in the morning. Then in June I'm finally free for summer.
dialogue.ru: В пятницу, в десять утра. А потом в июне я наконец свободен на всё лето.

dialogue.en: Nice. What are you doing at night before the exam?
dialogue.ru: Здорово. А что ты будешь делать ночью перед экзаменом?

dialogue.en: Sleeping, hopefully — not studying at midnight like last time.
dialogue.ru: Спать, надеюсь — а не готовиться в полночь, как в прошлый раз.

b1-l7.title: Present Perfect Continuous
b1-l7.explanation: This tense emphasizes the duration of an action that started in the past and either continues now or has just stopped, with visible results. "I have been learning English for three years" focuses on the ongoing process, not a finished result — compare it to present perfect simple ("I have learned English"), which focuses on the achievement itself.
b1-l7.fact: Present perfect continuous is often the tense native speakers reach for to explain a present clue with a past cause — "Your eyes are red, have you been crying?" — because it links an ongoing or recent activity directly to visible evidence in the moment.

dialogue.en: You look exhausted. Have you been working all night?
dialogue.ru: Ты выглядишь измотанной. Ты работала всю ночь?

dialogue.en: Yes, I've been preparing this presentation for two months, and it's finally due tomorrow.
dialogue.ru: Да, я готовлю эту презентацию уже два месяца, и завтра наконец сдавать.

dialogue.en: How long have you been sitting at that desk today?
dialogue.ru: Сколько ты сегодня сидишь за этим столом?

dialogue.en: Since six in the morning. I haven't been sleeping much lately.
dialogue.ru: С шести утра. В последнее время я мало сплю.

b1-l8.title: Relative Clauses
b1-l8.explanation: Relative clauses let you add extra detail about a noun without starting a new sentence. Use "who" for people, "which" for things, "that" for either (informally), "where" for places, and "whose" for possession: "the man whose car is parked here." This is how English packs description directly onto the noun instead of stacking short separate sentences.
b1-l8.fact: "That" can often replace "who" or "which" in casual speech, but not always — you can say "the book that I'm reading", but you can't drop "who"/"which" and use "that" right after a comma, which is why formal writing tends to prefer "who"/"which" over the more flexible "that."

dialogue.en: Is that the café where you had your interview?
dialogue.ru: Это то кафе, где у тебя было собеседование?

dialogue.en: Yes, and that's the manager who interviewed me, right there.
dialogue.ru: Да, и вон тот менеджер, который меня собеседовал.

dialogue.en: The one whose office is upstairs?
dialogue.ru: Тот, чей кабинет наверху?

dialogue.en: Exactly. He's the reason why I got the job, actually — he really liked my portfolio.
dialogue.ru: Именно. Он, кстати, и есть причина, почему я получил эту работу — ему очень понравилось моё портфолио.

b1-l9.title: Zero Conditional
b1-l9.explanation: Zero conditional describes things that are always true — scientific facts, natural laws, and automatic cause-and-effect: "If you heat water, it boils." Both clauses stay in present simple, and "if" can usually be swapped for "when" without changing the meaning, since it isn't really about a hypothetical situation — it's a rule that always holds.
b1-l9.fact: Because zero conditional describes guaranteed outcomes rather than possibilities, it's the conditional type used least often in casual conversation but most often in instructions, recipes, and science — anywhere the result is certain rather than merely likely.

dialogue.en: Why does the light flicker when I press this switch?
dialogue.ru: Почему свет мигает, когда я нажимаю на этот выключатель?

dialogue.en: If the wiring is old, the light flickers like that. It's normal in this building.
dialogue.ru: Если проводка старая, свет так мигает. Это нормально для этого здания.

dialogue.en: And if I turn it off completely?
dialogue.ru: А если я полностью его выключу?

dialogue.en: Then it stops, obviously. If you don't need the light, just leave it off.
dialogue.ru: Тогда, конечно, перестаёт. Если свет не нужен, просто оставь его выключенным.

b1-l10.title: Gerunds vs Infinitives
b1-l10.pattern: verb + -ing (enjoy, avoid, finish, keep...) · verb + to (want, decide, hope, agree...)
b1-l10.explanation: Some English verbs are followed by the -ing form (a gerund), others by "to + verb" (an infinitive), and unfortunately there's no single rule — you largely have to learn which pattern goes with which verb. Verbs about ongoing or completed activities (enjoy, avoid, finish, keep) usually take -ing; verbs about future intentions or decisions (want, decide, hope, plan, agree) usually take "to".
b1-l10.fact: A handful of verbs — like "stop", "remember", and "forget" — can take both forms but change meaning depending on which one you use: "I stopped smoking" (I quit) versus "I stopped to smoke" (I paused what I was doing in order to smoke), which makes this one of the few places in English grammar where the choice itself carries real information.

dialogue.en: Have you finished packing for the trip?
dialogue.ru: Ты закончила собирать вещи для поездки?

dialogue.en: Almost. I still need to buy a few things, but I've decided to travel light this time.
dialogue.ru: Почти. Мне ещё нужно кое-что купить, но в этот раз я решил путешествовать налегке.

dialogue.en: Good idea. I hate carrying heavy bags.
dialogue.ru: Хорошая идея. Ненавижу таскать тяжёлые сумки.

dialogue.en: Same. I really enjoy traveling when I'm not exhausted from the luggage.
dialogue.ru: Согласен. Мне действительно нравится путешествовать, когда я не измотан из-за багажа.
